title: Polynomial-time algorithm conjecture for the crossing-exchange-and-smoothing relation [42]
statement_text: For each fixed link $L$, there is a polynomial-time algorithm that solves $\succcurlyeq L$. That is, there is a polynomial $p(n)$ and an algorithm $A_L$ that takes as input any diagram $D$ with $n$ crossings, and answers whether or not $D \succcurlyeq L$ in at most $p(n)$ steps.
context_text: The relation $D \succcurlyeq L$ holds if there is a sequence of crossing exchanges and smoothings taking diagram $D$ to a diagram of link $L$. Reference [42] proved polynomial-time algorithms when $L$ is a torus link $T_{2,m}$ or a twist knot, and when $L$ is any prime link with crossing number at most 5, and conjectured that such algorithms exist for every fixed link $L$.
paper_contribution: Proves the conjecture completely: using the well-quasi-order of plane minors (Theorem 1.1) and an $O(n^2 \log n)$ sphere-minor testing algorithm (Theorem 1.3), the paper establishes that for each fixed link $L$ there is an $O(n^2 \log n)$ algorithm deciding whether $D \succcurlyeq L$ for any input diagram $D$ on $n$ crossings (Theorem 1.4).
notes: PDF source — the relation symbol is rendered as '(cid:59)' in the extraction and is likely a custom or Unicode symbol for the crossing-exchange-and-smoothing relation; approximated here as $\succcurlyeq$. The full identity of reference [42] cannot be determined from the available text (bibliography section is garbled in the PDF extraction).
paper_title: Well-quasi-order of plane minors and an application to link diagrams
